{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.9}}

Summary: Revised the draft to fix a likely-false novelty claim, repair the related-work taxonomy (missing MetaGPT/AutoGen/ChatDev, miscategorized ReAct), tighten the argument for typed communication, and qualify the headline 23% number.

## Key problems in the current draft

1. **Novelty claim is almost certainly wrong.** "The first multi-agent LLM framework to enforce typed inter-agent communication" overlooks MetaGPT (Hong et al., 2023), which already routes structured artifacts — PRDs, design docs, API specs, code — between role-specialized agents under an SOP that constrains document shape. AutoGen (Wu et al., 2023) supports typed messages and programmable validation between agents. ChatDev (Qian et al., 2023) uses phase-gated structured handoffs in a software-dev pipeline. Any of these is a direct precedent. The contribution needs to be re-scoped: e.g., "schema-validated handoffs with rejection-and-repair," "typed artifacts enforced by an external validator rather than by prompt convention," or "a formal type system over agent outputs." Whatever the actual delta is, state it precisely.

2. **Related-work taxonomy is miscalibrated for the paper's topic.** The paper is about multi-agent coordination on software-development tasks, but the three "threads" survey CAMEL (general role-play), Generative Agents (social simulation — not task-completion), and ReAct (explicitly single-agent, as the draft itself concedes). The genuinely relevant baselines — MetaGPT, AutoGen, ChatDev, AgentVerse (Chen et al., 2023), Self-collaboration Code Generation (Dong et al., 2023) — are absent. A reader from this subfield will notice immediately.

3. **The 23% headline is under-specified and the comparison is suspect.** "Strongest prior multi-agent baseline among the works surveyed above" — but none of the surveyed works (CAMEL, Generative Agents, HuggingGPT, ReAct) is a competitive software-development multi-agent system. If the actual baseline is one of those, the comparison is unfair; if it is MetaGPT/ChatDev, then the surveyed-works framing is wrong. Name the baseline, the benchmark, and the sample size.

4. **Argument slippage on what typing buys you.** The draft says validators "reject malformed handoffs," which is a structural guarantee, but then implies this prevents hallucination propagation and verifies preconditions, which are semantic properties. A JSON-schema-valid plan can still be a hallucinated plan. The introduction should either (a) restrict the claim to structural ambiguity at handoffs, or (b) describe the additional semantic checks (executability, type-checking, test-passing) that earn the stronger claim.

5. **"We believe this generalizes" is the weakest possible closing.** Either you have evidence of generalization (a second domain in the paper) or you should drop the sentence — it currently advertises an untested claim in the position readers weight most.

## Proposed revision

---

Multi-agent collaboration with large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a promising paradigm for problems that exceed a single prompt's capacity. By decomposing a task across role-specialized agents that exchange natural-language messages, recent systems report gains on planning, reasoning, and tool-use benchmarks over monolithic LLM baselines.

We group prior work into three threads relevant to coordinated task completion. First, **role-conditioned dialogue systems** — CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) and its descendants — show that role assignment alone, without explicit orchestration, can drive cooperative agents toward task progress. Second, **pipeline-style multi-agent software frameworks** — MetaGPT (Hong et al., 2023), ChatDev (Qian et al., 2023), and AutoGen (Wu et al., 2023) — assign agents to phases of a development workflow (requirements, design, implementation, review) and pass intermediate artifacts between them. Third, **tool-augmented single-agent controllers** — ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) and HuggingGPT (Shen et al., 2023) — interleave reasoning with API calls; we include them for context, but they orchestrate tools rather than peer agents. Behavior-grounded simulations such as Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) target social believability rather than task completion and lie outside our comparison set.

Across the first two threads, inter-agent communication is overwhelmingly conducted as free-form natural-language text. Even pipeline frameworks that pass nominally structured documents (e.g., MetaGPT's PRDs) rely on prompt-level convention rather than on machine-checked schemas, leaving handoffs vulnerable in three ways: (i) lexical ambiguity at the boundary — a "function name" field may appear in prose, in a code block, or be omitted; (ii) silent propagation of upstream hallucinations because no consumer verifies that what it received is well-formed; and (iii) no mechanism for the downstream agent to refuse a malformed artifact and request repair.

We propose **StructAgents**, a multi-agent framework in which every inter-agent boundary is mediated by a typed, schema-validated artifact and an external validator that rejects ill-formed handoffs and triggers repair. Agents emit and consume structured documents — task plans, interface specifications, code units, and test reports — with field-level types, referential integrity across artifacts (e.g., function signatures declared in the design must appear unchanged in the code), and optional semantic checks (parseability, type-checking, test execution) layered on top of structural validation.

Our contributions are: (1) a formalization of typed inter-agent handoffs that distinguishes structural validation (schema conformance) from semantic validation (executability against declared preconditions); (2) an open-source instantiation on a software-development task suite; and (3) an empirical comparison against MetaGPT, ChatDev, and AutoGen on [BENCHMARK, N tasks], where StructAgents improves end-to-end pass rate by 23 absolute points over the strongest baseline (MetaGPT, X% → Y%), with the largest gains concentrated on tasks whose downstream stages depend on precise upstream artifacts (e.g., cross-file API consistency).

We do not claim that typed communication is itself novel — MetaGPT and AutoGen already pass structured artifacts. The novel claim is narrower: that enforcing those types with an external validator and a repair loop, rather than via prompt convention, yields measurable improvements on dependency-heavy tasks.

---

## Notes on what I changed and why

- Rebuilt the related-work taxonomy around three threads that are actually relevant to coordinated task completion; moved Generative Agents out of the comparison set with a one-line justification; explicitly marked ReAct/HuggingGPT as single-agent.
- Added MetaGPT, ChatDev, and AutoGen as the real baselines.
- Replaced the "first to enforce typed communication" claim with a narrower, defensible novelty claim about external validation + repair loops.
- Sharpened the three failure modes of free-form handoffs into a concrete (i)/(ii)/(iii) list.
- Separated structural from semantic validation, addressing the earlier slippage.
- Made the 23% comparison name the baseline and benchmark (placeholders for you to fill in).
- Dropped the unsupported generalization claim from the closer; if you have a second-domain experiment, reinstate it with the evidence.

## Uncertainty

- I have not verified the citations (year, author list, venue) for MetaGPT, ChatDev, AutoGen, AgentVerse, or Dong et al.'s self-collaboration paper from primary sources in this turn; confirm before submission.
- The strong claim that MetaGPT uses "prompt convention" rather than machine-checked schemas reflects my understanding of its released implementation as of late 2023; if MetaGPT (or a later version) has since added formal validators, weaken the contrast accordingly.
- The placeholder baseline numbers (X%, Y%) are stubs — replace with your measured values, and confirm that MetaGPT really is the strongest of the three baselines on your suite before naming it.
